html:
V

Mrs.¬†Hableton Unbosoms Herself

Mrs.¬†Hableton was a lady with a grievance, as anybody who happened to become acquainted with her soon found out. It is Beaconsfield who says, in one of his novels, that no one is so interesting as when he is talking about himself; and, judging Mrs.¬†Hableton by this statement, she was an extremely fascinating individual, as she never by any chance talked upon any other subject. What was the threat of a Russian invasion to her so long as she had her special grievanceвБ†вАФonce let that be removed, and she would have time to attend to such minor details as affected the colony.

Mrs.¬†HabletonвАЩs particular grievance was want of money. Not by any means an uncommon one, you might remind her; but she snappishly would tell you that вАЬshe knowd that, but some people werenвАЩt like other people.вАЭ In time one came to learn what she meant by this. She had come to the Colonies in the early daysвБ†вАФdays when the making of money in appreciable quantity was an easier matter than it is now. Owing to a bad husband, she had failed to save any. The late Mr.¬†HabletonвБ†вАФfor he had long since departed this lifeвБ†вАФhad been addicted to alcohol, and at those times when he should have been earning, he was usually to be found in a drinking shanty spending his wifeвАЩs earnings in вАЬshoutingвАЭ for himself and his friends. The constant drinking, and the hot Victorian climate, soon carried him off, and when Mrs.¬†Hableton had seen him safely under the ground in the Melbourne Cemetery, she returned home to survey her position, and see how it could be bettered. She gathered together a little money from the wreck of her fortune, and land being cheap, purchased a small вАЬsectionвАЭ at St.¬†Kilda, and built a house on it. She supported herself by going out charing, taking in sewing, and acting as a sick nurse. So, among this multiplicity of occupations, she managed to exist fairly well.

And in truth it was somewhat hard upon Mrs.¬†Hableton. For at the time when she should have been resting and reaping the fruit of her early industry, she was obliged to toil more assiduously than ever. It was little consolation to her that she was but a type of many women, who, hardworking and thrifty themselves, are married to men who are nothing but an incubus to their wives and to their families. Small wonder, then, that Mrs.¬†Hableton should condense all her knowledge of the male sex into the one bitter aphorism, вАЬMen is brutes.вАЭ

Possum Villa was an unpretentious-looking place, with one bow-window and a narrow verandah in front. It was surrounded by a small garden in which were a few sparse flowersвБ†вАФthe especial delight of Mrs.¬†Hableton. It was her way to tie an old handkerchief round her head and to go out into the garden and dig and water her beloved flowers until, from sheer desperation at the overwhelming odds, they gave up all attempt to grow. She was engaged in this favourite occupation about a week after her lodger had gone. She wondered where he was.

вАЬLyinвАЩ drunk in a public-вАЩouse, IвАЩll be bound,вАЭ she said, viciously pulling up a weed, вАЬa-spendinвАЩ вАЩis, rent and a-spilinвАЩ вАЩis inside with beerвБ†вАФah, men is brutes, drat вАЩem!вАЭ

Just as she said this, a shadow fell across the garden, and on looking up, she saw a man leaning over the fence, staring at her.

вАЬGit out,вАЭ she said, sharply, rising from her knees and shaking her trowel at the intruder. вАЬI donвАЩt want no apples today, anвАЩ I donвАЩt care how cheap you sells вАЩem.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton evidently laboured under the delusion that the man was a hawker, but seeing no handcart with him, she changed her mind.

вАЬYouвАЩre takinвАЩ a plan of the вАЩouse to rob it, are you?вАЭ she said. вАЬWell, you neednвАЩt, вАЩcause there ainвАЩt nothinвАЩ to rob, the silver spoons as belonged to my fatherвАЩs mother вАЩavinвАЩ gone down my вАЩusbandвАЩs throat long ago, anвАЩ I ainвАЩt вАЩad money to buy more. IвАЩm a lone pusson as is put on by brutes like you, anвАЩ IвАЩll thank you to leave the fence I bought with my own вАЩard earned money alone, and git out.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton stopped short for want of breath, and stood shaking her trowel, and gasping like a fish out of water.

вАЬMy dear lady,вАЭ said the man at the fence, mildly, вАЬare youвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬNo, I ainвАЩt,вАЭ retorted Mrs.¬†Hableton, fiercely, вАЬI ainвАЩt neither a member of the вАЩOuse, nor a school teacher, to answer your questions. IвАЩm a woman as pays my rates anвАЩ taxes, and donвАЩt gossip nor read yer rubbishinвАЩ newspapers, nor care for the Russings, no how, so git out.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt read the papers?вАЭ repeated the man, in a satisfied tone, вАЬah! that accounts for it.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton stared suspiciously at the intruder. He was a burly-looking man, with a jovial red face, clean shaven, and his sharp, shrewd-looking grey eyes twinkled like two stars. He was well-dressed in a suit of light clothes, and wore a stiffly-starched white waistcoat, with a massive gold chain stretched across it. Altogether he gave Mrs.¬†Hableton finally the impression of being a well-to-do tradesman, and she mentally wondered what he wanted.

вАЬWhat dвАЩy want?вАЭ she asked, abruptly.

вАЬDoes Mr.¬†Oliver Whyte live here?вАЭ asked the stranger.

вАЬHe do, anвАЩ he donвАЩt,вАЭ answered Mrs.¬†Hableton, epigrammatically. вАЬI ainвАЩt seen вАЩim for over a week, so I sвАЩpose вАЩeвАЩs gone on the drink, like the rest of вАЩem, but IвАЩve put sumthinвАЩ in the paper as вАЩill pull him up pretty sharp, and let вАЩim know I ainвАЩt a carpet to be trod on, anвАЩ if youвАЩre a friend of вАЩim, you can tell вАЩim from me вАЩeвАЩs a brute, anвАЩ itвАЩs no more but what I expected of вАЩim, вАЩe beinвАЩ a male.вАЭ

The stranger waited placidly during the outburst, and Mrs.¬†Hableton, having stopped for want of breath, he interposed, quietlyвБ†вАФ

вАЬCan I speak to you for a few moments?вАЭ

вАЬAnвАЩ whoвАЩs a-stoppinвАЩ of you?вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton, defiantly. вАЬGo on with you, not as I expects the truth from a male, but go on.вАЭ

вАЬWell, really,вАЭ said the other, looking up at the cloudless blue sky, and wiping his face with a gaudy red silk pocket-handkerchief, вАЬit is rather hot, you know, andвБ†вАФвАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton did not give him time to finish, but walking to the gate, opened it with a jerk.

вАЬUse your legs and walk in,вАЭ she said, and the stranger having done so, she led the way into the house, and into a small neat sitting-room, which seemed to overflow with antimacassars, wool mats, and wax flowers. There were also a row of emu eggs on the mantelpiece, a cutlass on the wall, and a grimy line of hard-looking little books, set in a stiff row on a shelf, presumably for ornament, for their appearance in no way tempted one to read them.

The furniture was of horsehair, and everything was hard and shiny, so when the stranger sat down in the slippery-looking armchair that Mrs.¬†Hableton pushed towards him, he could not help thinking it had been stuffed with stones, it felt so cold and hard. The lady herself sat opposite to him in another hard chair, and having taken the handkerchief off her head, folded it carefully, laid it on her lap, and then looked straight at her unexpected visitor.

вАЬNow then,вАЭ she said, letting her mouth fly open so rapidly that it gave one the impression that it was moved by strings like a marionette, вАЬWho are you? what are you? and what do you want?вАЭ

The stranger put his red silk handkerchief into his hat, placed it on the table, and answered deliberatelyвБ†вАФ

вАЬMy name is Gorby. I am a detective. I want Mr.¬†Oliver Whyte.вАЭ

вАЬHe ainвАЩt here,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton, thinking that Whyte had got into trouble, and was in danger of arrest.

вАЬI know that,вАЭ answered Mr.¬†Gorby.

вАЬThen where is вАЩe?вАЭ

Mr.¬†Gorby answered abruptly, and watched the effect of his words.

вАЬHe is dead.вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton grew pale, and pushed back her chair. вАЬNo,вАЭ she cried, вАЬhe never killed вАЩim, did вАЩe?вАЭ

вАЬWho never killed him?вАЭ queried Mr.¬†Gorby, sharply.

Mrs.¬†Hableton evidently knew more than she intended to say, for, recovering herself with a violent effort, she answered evasivelyвБ†вАФ

вАЬHe never killed himself.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Gorby looked at her keenly, and she returned his gaze with a defiant stare.

вАЬClever,вАЭ muttered the detective to himself; вАЬknows something more than she chooses to tell, but IвАЩll get it out of her.вАЭ He paused a moment, and then went on smoothly:

вАЬOh, no! he did not commit suicide; what makes you think so?вАЭ Mrs.¬†Hableton did not answer, but, rising from her seat, went over to a hard and shiny-looking sideboard, from whence she took a bottle of brandy and a small wineglass. Half filling the glass, she drank it off, and returned to her seat.

вАЬI donвАЩt take much of that stuff,вАЭ she said, seeing the detectiveвАЩs eyes fixed curiously on her, вАЬbut you вАЩave given me such a turn that I must take something to steady my nerves; what do you want me to do?вАЭ

вАЬTell me all you know,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Gorby, keeping his eyes fixed on her face.

вАЬWhere was Mr.¬†Whyte killed?вАЭ she asked.

вАЬHe was murdered in a hansom cab on the St.¬†Kilda Road.вАЭ

вАЬIn the open street?вАЭ she asked in a startled tone.

вАЬYes, in the open street.вАЭ

вАЬAh!вАЭ she drew a long breath, and closed her lips, firmly. Mr.¬†Gorby said nothing. He saw that she was deliberating whether or not to speak, and a word from him might seal her lips, so, like a wise man, he kept silent. He obtained his reward sooner than he expected.

вАЬMr.¬†Gorby,вАЭ she said at length, вАЬI вАЩave вАЩad a вАЩard struggle all my life, which it came along of a bad husband, who was a brute and a drunkard, so, God knows, I ainвАЩt got much inducement to think well of the lot of you, butвБ†вАФmurder,вАЭ she shivered slightly, though the room was quite warm, вАЬI didnвАЩt think of that.вАЭ

вАЬIn connection with whom?вАЭ

вАЬMr.¬†Whyte, of course,вАЭ she answered, hurriedly.

вАЬAnd who else?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know.вАЭ

вАЬThen there is nobody else?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I donвАЩt knowвБ†вАФIвАЩm not sure.вАЭ

The detective was puzzled.

вАЬWhat do you mean?вАЭ he asked.

вАЬI will tell you all I know,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton, вАЬanвАЩ if вАЩeвАЩs innocent, God will вАЩelp вАЩim.вАЭ

вАЬIf who is innocent?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll tell you everythinвАЩ from the start,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton, вАЬanвАЩ you can judge for yourself.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Gorby assented, and she began:

вАЬItвАЩs only two months ago since I decided to take in lodgers; but charinвАЩs вАЩard work, and sewinвАЩs tryinвАЩ for the eyes, so, beinвАЩ a lone woman, вАЩavinвАЩ bin badly treated by a brute, who is now dead, which I was allays a good wife to вАЩim, I thought lodgers вАЩud вАЩelp me a little, so I put a notice in the paper, anвАЩ Mr.¬†Oliver Whyte took the rooms two months ago.вАЭ

вАЬWhat was he like?вАЭ

вАЬNot very tall, dark face, no whiskers nor moustache, anвАЩ quite the gentleman.вАЭ

вАЬAnything peculiar about him?вАЭ

Mrs.¬†Hableton thought for a moment.

вАЬWell,вАЭ she said at length, вАЬhe вАЩad a mole on his left temple, but it was covered with вАЩis вАЩair, anвАЩ few people вАЩud вАЩave seen it.вАЭ

вАЬThe very man,вАЭ said Gorby to himself, вАЬIвАЩm on the right path.вАЭ

вАЬMr.¬†Whyte said вАЩe вАЩad just come from England,вАЭ went on the woman.

вАЬWhich,вАЭ thought Mr.¬†Gorby, вАЬaccounts for the corpse not being recognised by friends.вАЭ

вАЬHe took the rooms, anвАЩ said вАЩeвАЩd stay with me for six months, anвАЩ paid a weekвАЩs rent in advance, anвАЩ вАЩe allays paid up regвАЩler like a respectable man, though I donвАЩt believe in вАЩem myself. He said вАЩeвАЩd lots of friends, anвАЩ used to go out every night.вАЭ

вАЬWho were his friends?вАЭ

вАЬThat I canвАЩt tell you, for вАЩe were very close, anвАЩ when вАЩe went out of doors I never knowd where вАЩe went, which is jest like вАЩem; for they ses theyвАЩre goinвАЩ to work, anвАЩ you finds вАЩem in the beershop. Mr.¬†Whyte told me вАЩe was a-goinвАЩ to marry a heiress, вАЩe was.вАЭ

вАЬAh!вАЭ interjected Mr.¬†Gorby, sapiently.

вАЬHe вАЩad only one friend as I ever sawвБ†вАФa Mr.¬†MorelandвБ†вАФwho comed вАЩere with вАЩm, anвАЩ was allays with вАЩimвБ†вАФbrother-like.вАЭ

вАЬWhat is this Mr.¬†Moreland like?вАЭ

вАЬGood-lookinвАЩ enough,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton sourly, вАЬbut вАЩis вАЩabits werenвАЩt as good as вАЩis faceвБ†вАФвАЩandsom is as вАЩandsom does, is what I ses.вАЭ

вАЬI wonder if he knows anything about this affair,вАЭ thought Gorby to himself. вАЬWhere is Mr.¬†Moreland to be found?вАЭ he asked.

вАЬNot knowinвАЩ, canвАЩt tell,вАЭ retorted the landlady, вАЬвАКвАЩe used to be вАЩere regвАЩlar, but I ainвАЩt seen вАЩim for over a week.вАЭ

вАЬStrange! very!вАЭ said Gorby, shaking his head. вАЬI should like to see this Mr.¬†Moreland. I suppose itвАЩs probable heвАЩll call again?вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩAbit beinвАЩ second nature I sвАЩpose he will,вАЭ answered the woman, вАЬвАКвАЩe might call at any time, mostly вАЩavinвАЩ called at night.вАЭ

вАЬAh! then IвАЩll come down this evening on chance of seeing him,вАЭ replied the detective. вАЬCoincidences happen in real life as well as in novels, and the gentleman in question may turn up in the nick of time. Now, what else about Mr.¬†Whyte?вАЭ

вАЬAbout two weeks ago, or three, IвАЩm not certвАЩin which, a gentleman called to see Mr.¬†Whyte; вАЩe was very tall, and wore a light coat.вАЭ

вАЬAh! a morning coat?вАЭ

вАЬNo! вАЩe was in eveninвАЩ dress, and wore a light coat over it, anвАЩ a soft вАЩat.вАЭ

вАЬThe very man,вАЭ said the detective below his breath; вАЬgo on.вАЭ

вАЬHe went into Mr.¬†WhyteвАЩs room, anвАЩ shut the door. I donвАЩt know how long they were talkinвАЩ together; but I was sittinвАЩ in this very room and heard their voices git angry, and they were a-swearinвАЩ at one another, which is the way with men, the brutes. I got up and went into the passage in order to ask вАЩem not to make such a noise, when Mr.¬†WhyteвАЩs door opens, anвАЩ the gentleman in the light coat comes out, and bangs along to the door. Mr.¬†Whyte вАЩe comes to the door of вАЩis room, anвАЩ вАЩe вАЩollers out. вАШShe is mine; you canвАЩt do anything; anвАЩ the other turns with вАЩis вАЩand on the door anвАЩ says, вАШI can kill you, anвАЩ if you marry вАЩer IвАЩll do it, even in the open street.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬAh!вАЭ said Mr.¬†Gorby, drawing a long breath, вАЬand then?вАЭ

вАЬThen he bangs the door to, which itвАЩs never shut easy since, anвАЩ I ainвАЩt got no money to get it put right, anвАЩ Mr.¬†Whyte walks back to his room, laughing.вАЭ

вАЬDid he make any remark to you?вАЭ

вАЬNo; except heвАЩd been worried by a loonatic.вАЭ

вАЬAnd what was the strangerвАЩs name?вАЭ

вАЬThat I canвАЩt tell you, as Mr.¬†Whyte never told me. He was very tall, with a fair moustache, anвАЩ dressed as I told you.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Gorby was satisfied.

вАЬThat is the man,вАЭ he said to himself, вАЬwho got into the hansom cab, and murdered Whyte; thereвАЩs no doubt of it! Whyte and he were rivals for the heiress.вАЭ

вАЬWhat dвАЩy think of it?вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Hableton curiously.

вАЬI think,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Gorby slowly, with his eyes fixed on her, вАЬI think that there is a woman at the bottom of this crime.вАЭ
